title: Snyk keys should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Snyk API token detected in source code. Snyk API tokens provide programmatic access to Snyk's security platform, enabling vulnerability scanning, dependency analysis, container security testing, and infrastructure-as-code (IaC) security assessments. Exposing these tokens creates severe security risks: attackers can access your organization's vulnerability reports and security findings, view details about all security issues in your projects, modify project settings and security policies, access dependency trees and component details, scan and analyze your codebase and infrastructure, consume API quotas and rate limits, disable security gates and checks, modify ignore rules and vulnerability suppressions, access integration configurations, view license compliance information, and potentially access CI/CD pipeline configurations. Snyk tokens often have broad permissions across multiple projects and organizations. This is a critical security vulnerability requiring immediate remediation. Take immediate action: revoke the exposed token immediately from Snyk dashboard (Account Settings > API Tokens), generate a new API token with minimal necessary permissions, update all applications, CI/CD pipelines, and integrations with the new token, review Snyk audit logs for any unauthorized access or suspicious activity, check which projects and organizations the exposed token had access to, audit all security policy changes made recently, verify no security gates or checks were disabled, review all ignore rules and vulnerability suppressions for tampering, implement principle of least privilege for token permissions (use project-specific tokens when possible), store Snyk tokens in secure secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault), use environment variables with restricted access for CI/CD pipelines, never commit Snyk tokens to version control or expose in configuration files, implement secret scanning in version control and CI/CD pipelines, use Snyk service accounts with appropriate scopes for automation, rotate Snyk tokens regularly (every 90 days recommended), enable SSO and enforce MFA for Snyk accounts, monitor Snyk API usage for anomalies, document all Snyk tokens in a secure credential inventory, use Snyk's webhook integration for real-time security notifications instead of polling APIs when possible, and educate development teams about proper token handling and the sensitivity of security scanning credentials.
